elements.sent-2411-001: Hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) hosts quantum emitters that exhibit single-photon emission and spin-dependent fluorescence at room temperature.
elements.sent-2411-002: These features make hBN a promising platform for quantum sensing and photonics.
elements.sent-2411-003: Despite many investigations of their optical properties, the emitters’ chemical structure remains unclear, as does the role of contamination at surfaces and interfaces in forming the emitters or modifying their properties.
elements.sent-2411-004: We prepare hBN samples that are compatible with both confocal photoluminescence microscopy (PL) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and we use those techniques to investigate correlations between fluorescent emission, flake morphology, and surface residue.
elements.sent-2411-005: We find that the microscopy techniques themselves induce changes in hBN's optical activity and residue morphology: PL measurements induce photobleaching, whereas TEM measurements alter surface residue and emission characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also study the effects of common treatments\,---\,annealing and oxygen plasma cleaning\,---\,on the structure and optical activity of hBN.
elements.sent-2411-007: The results illustrate the power and importance of correlative studies to elucidate aspects of microscopic mechanisms that influence hBN's functionality as a host for quantum emitters and spin defects.
elements.sent-2411-008: Hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) is a van der Waals material with utility across many applications.
elements.sent-2411-009: For example, given that hBN is a wide-bandgap semiconductor ($\sim$5.9 eV) \cite{Shaik2021OpticalSources,Jungwirth2016 , it is frequently used as an insulating layer in electronic devices \cite{Dean2010BoronElectronics,Yu2013InteractionCapacitance,Kuiri2015ProbingMeasurements .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: More recently, hBN has emerged as an exciting candidate for use in quantum information technologies, as the material hosts bright quantum emitters at room temperature \cite{Tran2016b,Exarhos2017,Mendelson2019EngineeringNitride,Patel2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Some of these emitters display spin-dependent properties and may therefore be utilized as spin qubits \cite{Exarhos2019,Chejanovsky2021,Guo2023CoherentTemperature,Patel2024RoomNitride,Stern2024AConditions .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, these quantum emitters are not yet fully understood.
elements.sent-2411-013: Identifying emitters is particularly complex because they are sensitive to interfaces with other materials \cite{Exarhos2017,Akbari2021 and local environmental effects (e.g., defects \cite{Chejanovsky2021 , strain \cite{Proscia2018,Sajid2020 , and contamination \cite{Yang2022EffectMaterials,Li2023ProlongedEmitters ), which are especially pertinent concerns when utilizing any 2D material.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Understanding and controlling the properties of hBN, particularly at its surface, are therefore crucial to advancing quantum information and photonics applications.
elements.sent-2411-015: Techniques such as photoluminescence (PL) microscopy and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) have previously been employed to understand and create quantum emitters in hBN.
elements.sent-2411-016: PL provides valuable information about optical properties, including absorption and emission characteristics, while offering insight into chemical and electronic information \cite{Jungwirth2016,Wigger2019Phonon-assistedNitride .}
elements.sent-2411-017: SEM has been used to study surface topography and morphology \cite{Exarhos2017,Mendelson2019EngineeringNitride , in addition to activating emission centers in hBN by electron irradiation \cite{Tran2016b,Breitweiser2020,Fournier2021Position-controlledNitride .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: More recently, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has been used as a means of studying quantum emission in hBN when paired with PL or cathodoluminescence spectroscopy \cite{Hayee2020,Li2023ProlongedEmitters .}
elements.sent-2411-019: TEMs enable direct observation of material structure down to the atomic level and are generally operated at much higher accelerating voltages than SEMs ($\sim$80--200 kV versus $\sim$5--30 kV), opening up a new regime of irradiation conditions to be explored for the possible creation of emitters \cite{Alem2009AtomicallyMicroscopy,Bui2023CreationIrradiation,Keneipp2024NanoscaleBeam .}
elements.sent-2411-020: While the optical properties and material structure of hBN are well-studied separately, few studies have investigated them in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-021: The absence of studies employing both TEM and PL microscopy can be partially attributed to the disparity in scale at which these two techniques operate.
elements.sent-2411-022: Confocal PL studies in the visible-wavelength range are typically limited to a resolution of $\sim$300 nm, whereas TEM studies can achieve atomic resolution.
elements.sent-2411-023: By combining both of these techniques, the capabilities of TEM can be leveraged to help determine the structural and chemical composition of quantum emitters identified via PL microscopy.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/Fig1.png} \caption{ Summary of Experimental Methods All samples undergo an iterative process that includes basic characterization, correlative PL and TEM measurements, and treatments. (a) All samples are first characterized with white light imaging and AFM. The white light image shows Sample A, a mechanically exfoliated flake of hBN (outlined in purple) suspended over a 10 $\mu$m diameter hole milled in a silicon nitride membrane. (b) Basic characterization is followed by PL and/or TEM measurements. The schematics illustrate a cross-sectional view of each correlated microscopy technique. (c) Some samples then receive annealing and/or oxygen plasma irradiation treatments. Changes in optical activity and morphology following treatment are analyzed via the correlated microscopies and basic characterization methods.} \label{fig:Schematic}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this study, we combine PL and TEM imaging to bridge the gap in understanding between hBN's optical properties and material structure.
elements.sent-2411-025: We focus on changes in overall optical activity and flake morphology associated with different measurements and treatments, rather than investigating single-photon emitters with PL or few-atom defects with TEM.
elements.sent-2411-026: To this end, we fabricate custom substrates compatible with both microscopies onto which hBN flakes ranging from $\sim$10 to $\sim$30 nm thick, as measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM), are transferred.
elements.sent-2411-027: First, we consider the effects of PL and TEM measurements themselves on hBN, which is particularly important because of the iterative approach of the measurements.
elements.sent-2411-028: We show that exposure to the electron beam during TEM imaging induces changes to contaminants present on flakes and that PL imaging induces photobleaching.
elements.sent-2411-029: Then, we demonstrate the utility of this protocol by studying correlated changes in structure and optical activity following annealing and oxygen plasma irradiation, treatments commonly applied to hBN \cite{Li2019PurificationTreatments, Li2023ProlongedEmitters, Venturi2024SelectiveNitride, Shaik2021OpticalSources,Mohajerani2024NarrowbandNitride, Chen2023AnnealingNitride .}
elements.sent-2411-030: We find that both treatments significantly alter sample morphology and optical activity.
elements.sent-2411-031: Each sample discussed in this paper received a distinct sequence of measurements and treatments which are summarized in Table \TabSampleList\ in the Supporting Information.
elements.sent-2411-032: The unique procedure introduced here facilitates a comprehensive study of quantum emission in hBN, revealing the detailed and complex interplay between material structure and optical activity and the effects of common treatments.
elements.sent-2411-033: Furthermore, this approach can be easily adapted to other 2D materials, demonstrating its broad utility for advancing the field as a whole.
elements.sent-2411-034: Each sample discussed in this paper received a distinct sequence of measurements and treatments that are summarized in Table~\TabSampleList\ in the Supporting Information.
elements.sent-2411-035: Figure~ ref{fig:Schematic summarizes the different characterization methods and treatments we explore.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Sample preparation begins by mechanically exfoliating flakes of hBN from bulk crystal (HQ Graphene) and using polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) viscoelastic stamping to transfer flakes onto 100$\times$100 $\mu$m$^2$ silicon nitride (SiN$_x$) windows.
elements.sent-2411-037: To improve TEM resolution and ensure that PL measurements are not subject to substrate induced effects, hBN flakes are suspended over holes patterned in SiN$_x$ windows via focused ion beam (FIB) milling.
elements.sent-2411-038: A 5- to 10-$\mu$m-diameter FIB hole (Fig.~\ref{fig:Schematic a) provides a sufficiently large suspended region of material suitable for both TEM and PL microscopy, determined by the diffraction limit of the laser.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Smaller, 2-$\mu$m-diameter FIB holes (Fig.~\ref{fig:Schematic a) help define the sample orientation and provide an area over which the TEM can initially be aligned.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Flake thickness is another important consideration.
elements.sent-2411-041: Monolayer or few-layer flakes are best for TEM studies at atomic resolution.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, stable single-photon emitters in hBN tend to occur in thicker flakes that are typically at least 30 nm thick \cite{Tran2016,Ngoc2018EffectsNitride .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Therefore, we utilized flakes of intermediate thickness, ranging from $\sim$10 nm to $\sim$30 nm, to meet the needs of both microscopies.
elements.sent-2411-044: Flakes of the desired thickness were identified by optical contrast prior to transferring and subsequently confirmed with AFM (see Table \TabSampleList\ in the Supporting Information).

elements.sent-2411-045: Figure \ref{fig:fig2 demonstrates the effect of electron beam irradiation on a representative region of Sample A over time.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Figure \ref{fig:fig2 b shows that this region of Sample A is covered with a uniform film of residue.}
elements.sent-2411-047: However, with prolonged exposure to the electron beam ($\sim$20 nA probe current), the residue changes in structure and density.
elements.sent-2411-048: Qualitatively, the residue appears to clump together and diminish over time, resulting in the lacey network of residue shown in Figure \ref{fig:fig2 d after 40 minutes of irradiation.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Upon analyzing the changes in contamination coverage and distribution of contaminated areas by size, we found that at 0, 25, and 40 minutes, residue coverage was 23\%, 25\%, and 19\%, respectively (see Figs.~\FigSTwo\ and \FigSThree\ in the Supporting Information for a detailed description of this analysis).
elements.sent-2411-050: Histograms of contaminated areas reveal that the average size of contaminated follows a similar trend: average size increases between 0 and 25 minutes, then decreases (Fig.~\ref{fig:fig2 e-g).}
elements.sent-2411-051: The overall decrease in contamination coverage and average size between 0 and 40 minutes of irradiation shows that prolonged exposure to the electron beam has a cleaning effect, while the increase between 0 and 25 minutes implies that this effect is dose-dependent.
elements.sent-2411-052: It has been shown that hydrocarbons tend to diffuse across the surface of samples towards the electron beam where they are eventually polymerized and fixed in place, often covering features of interest \cite{Egerton2004RadiationSEM,Rykaczewski2007AnalysisMicroscopy .}
elements.sent-2411-053: On the other hand, electron irradiation can also remove material through knock-on damage, radiolysis, or charging effects \cite{Egerton2019RadiationTEM .}
elements.sent-2411-054: These competing phenomena explain the non-monotonic change in residue on Sample A.
elements.sent-2411-055: The residue shown in Figure \ref{fig:fig2 b-d likely arises from the PDMS used to transfer hBN flakes.}
elements.sent-2411-056: A wide body of literature describes how contamination arising from polymer-assisted transfer techniques of 2D materials is a pervasive issue for the field, and hBN is no exception \cite{Bhatia2024ANitride, Cheliotis2024AMaterials, Jain2018MinimizingPDMS, Jang2023AGraphene .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDS) maps confirm the presence of silicon, carbon and oxygen species, which is consistent with the composition of PDMS (see Fig.~\ref{fig:fig2 h-j and Fig.~\FigEDSSpectra\ in the Supporting Information) and previous work \cite{Bhatia2024ANitride .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Moreover, all three maps appear to be spatially correlated with the contaminated (dark) areas in the corresponding brightfield scanning TEM (BF STEM) image (Fig.~\ref{fig:fig2 k).}
elements.sent-2411-059: In an effort to understand how electron irradiation and changes to surface contaminants impact optical activity, Sample A was also studied with PL microscopy before and after TEM imaging.
elements.sent-2411-060: It is worth noting that the 80kV accelerating voltage used here exceeds the knock-on energies of boron and nitrogen \cite{Keneipp2024NanoscaleBeam, Kotakoski2010ElectronMonolayers, Dai2023EvolutionNitride, Gilbert2017FabricationNitride .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Therefore when considering the effect of electron irradiation on optical activity, we cannot rule out the possibility that a combination of changes in contamination structure and changes in hBN's lattice may both play a role.

elements.sent-2411-088: In this section, we established a foundation for correlating PL and TEM measurements and explored the self-generated effects of each microscopy technique.
elements.sent-2411-089: We found that the order of PL and TEM measurements is crucial because electron irradiation in the TEM results in significant changes to the optical properties of a given hBN sample.
elements.sent-2411-090: Our approach has implications for future work studying defect structures that give rise to emission or future work investigating how different hBN preparation methods influence optical signals and surface residue.
elements.sent-2411-091: Thus far, we have demonstrated a method of studying the same sample using TEM and PL microscopy.
elements.sent-2411-092: In order to avoid the effect of electron irradiation on optical activity, these microscopies should instead be correlated across different but comparable samples.
elements.sent-2411-093: We demonstrate the utility of this approach in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-094: We perform two treatments (oxygen plasma irradiation and annealing) on hBN and study the resulting optical and structural changes.
elements.sent-2411-095: Oxygen plasma irradiation and annealing are commonly used to improve quantum emission in hBN (e.g.~increasing brightness and density of emitters) \cite{Breitweiser2020,Tran2016b,Fischer2021 , but their effects on the morphology of the material are not well understood.}
elements.sent-2411-096: In Section \ref{sec:treatment , we investigate Samples B--E.}
elements.sent-2411-097: Since these samples are all between $\sim$10 and $\sim$20 nm thick (see Table \TabThickness\ in the Supporting Information) and share identical fabrication and treatment protocols, we are justified in correlating the results of TEM and PL measurements across these four samples.

elements.sent-2411-099: Sample B was irradiated with oxygen plasma for 5 minutes (50 W, 50 sccm O$_2$).
elements.sent-2411-100: TEM images show that on the micron scale, this treatment smoothed wrinkles on the flake (Fig.~\ref{fig:fig4 a,b) but had no other obvious effects.}
elements.sent-2411-101: On the nanometer scale, however, oxygen plasma irradiation significantly reduced contamination coverage on Sample B from 25\% to 7\%, with a similar decrease in average contaminant area size (Fig.~\ref{fig:fig4 c,d and \FigSFour).}
elements.sent-2411-102: This treatment also resulted in significant etching of Sample B (15 $\pm$ 2 nm $\rightarrow$ 9 $\pm$ 2 nm) and a decrease in surface roughness by a factor of approximately 2 (see Tables \TabThickness\ and \TabRoughness).
elements.sent-2411-103: This result was unsurprising, given that oxygen plasma treatment is regularly employed as a cleaning method \cite{Na2021ModulationTreatment, Jadwiszczak2021PlasmaApplications, Kim2016Effectssub2/sub .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Sample C was annealed for 2 hours at 850\textdegree in an argon environment.
elements.sent-2411-105: TEM images show that annealing resulted in significant cracking and warping of the flake (Fig.~\ref{fig:fig4 i,j and \FigSFive) and appeared to increase contamination from 7\% to 23\% in the regions shown in panels (k) and (l).}
elements.sent-2411-106: Although, localized, sub-micron holes appeared after treatment, annealing did not result in significant thinning of Sample C (Fig.~\ref{fig:fig4 o,p).}
elements.sent-2411-107: Annealing also resulted in an increase in roughness by a factor of 2.5 in Sample C (see Table \TabRoughness).
elements.sent-2411-108: The introduction of cracks and warping, alongside the increase in roughness from annealing is notable.
elements.sent-2411-109: The development such features may be the result of differing rates of expansion and contraction during heating and cooling between the hBN flake and the underlying SiN$_x$ membrane \cite{Zhang2023TheAnnealing .}
elements.sent-2411-110: Similarly, the increase in contamination coverage (7\% $\rightarrow$ 23\%) and average contaminant area (18 nm$^2$ to 56 nm$^2$, see Fig. \FigSFive) was unexpected because annealing is also often used as a cleaning technique \cite{Garcia2012EffectiveDevices, Lin2012GrapheneBe .}

elements.sent-2411-149: Another explanation for the dimming effect is defect migration and/or transmutation, which potentially reduces emission or blue-shifts the emission wavelength to become undetectable in our experimental setup \cite{Venturi2024SelectiveNitride,Weston2018NativeNitride .}
elements.sent-2411-150: Emitter family analysis does not reveal a clear shift in all families following annealing, suggesting significant chemical changes occur during this process.
elements.sent-2411-151: One possible evolution pattern is depicted by the arrows in Figure \ref{fig:PL-treatments j.}
elements.sent-2411-152: In conclusion, we developed a method for sequential, correlated TEM and PL measurements of multi-layer hBN, and we used these complementary techniques, along with AFM and white light imaging, to study the interplay between surface chemistry, contamination, overall morphology, thickness, and optical properties of mechanically exfoliated hBN\,---\,all critical considerations in the use of hBN in scalable quantum information and photonics technologies.
elements.sent-2411-153: We show that viscoelastic stamping with PDMS results in considerable residue on the surface of hBN flakes through direct TEM imaging and elemental mapping.
elements.sent-2411-154: We observe that this residue undergoes non-monotonic changes in structure as a result of electron irradiation.
elements.sent-2411-155: We also observe that oxygen plasma treatment results in thinning of hBN and reduces surface residue, but that annealing does not have the same effect.
elements.sent-2411-156: Instead, annealing induces warping and cracking in hBN flakes and results in a re-distribution of surface residue.
elements.sent-2411-157: Furthermore, we show how the optical properties of hBN change as a result of TEM irradiation, oxygen plasma, annealing, and PL measurement itself.
elements.sent-2411-158: In this intermediate thickness regime, all optical emission is subject to photobleaching, and irradiation by TEM or oxygen plasma tends to brighten and diffuse PL emission.
elements.sent-2411-159: The annealing treatment consistently reduces optical activity.
elements.sent-2411-160: These qualitative observations are confirmed by the results of emitter family analysis.
elements.sent-2411-161: This work sets the stage for further studies on hBN.
elements.sent-2411-162: Such work could include a systematic comparison of different sample preparation methods (i.e.~dry transfer, wet transfer, etc.) and the resulting residue or lack thereof; a systematic comparison of the effect of thickness on optical activity (if any); or potentially investigation of strain-induced effects by utilizing more complex substrate geometries.
elements.sent-2411-163: Motivated by the promise of hBN in quantum information and photonics applications, the method introduced in this work may also be adapted to a broad range of 2D materials.
elements.sent-2411-164: TEM grids were purchased from SiMPore with 50-nm-thick SiN$_x$ membranes suspended above silicon supports.
elements.sent-2411-165: The suspended SiN$_x$ region was 100 $\times$ 100 $\mu$m large.
elements.sent-2411-166: Holes of varying sizes (1, 5, and 10 $\mu$m diameter) were drilled in the SiN membranes with a Xe$^+$ ion beam in the Tescan S8252X plasma focused ion beam scanning electron microscope (PFIB-SEM).
elements.sent-2411-167: The PFIB was operated at 15 kV and 300 pA for drilling and the SEM at 5 kV and 100 pA for imaging of SiN memebranes.
elements.sent-2411-168: Once patterned with holes of the desired size and location, we mechanically exfoliated hBN (HQ Graphene) and transferred multi-layer flakes onto the SiN membranes with PDMS stamping.
elements.sent-2411-169: This procedure has been previously reported; see Bhatia et al., 2024 and Keneipp et al., 2024 for more details.
elements.sent-2411-170: AFM measurements were performed using a Bruker Icon AFM.
elements.sent-2411-171: To obtain values for flake thickness and roughness, regions of the flake comparable in thickness (as determined by white-light imaging) to the suspended region were scanned.
elements.sent-2411-172: In general, AFM scans improved when taken areas of the flake supported by the silicon substrate, instead of regions of the flake on the SiN$_x$ membrane, as the thin SiN$_x$ membrane resulted in a strong, nonuniform background signal in the AFM scans.
elements.sent-2411-173: Additional information about AFM data processing are provided in the Supporting Information.
elements.sent-2411-174: PL images were collected using a custom-built confocal microscope with a 592 nm continuous-wave laser (MPB Communications, VF-P-200- 592).
elements.sent-2411-175: The sample was illuminated with circularly polarized light at 100 $\mu$W unless otherwise noted.
elements.sent-2411-176: A long-pass filter with a cut-on wavelength of 650 nm (Semrock, BLP01- 635R-25) is placed in the collection path to eliminate scattered light.
elements.sent-2411-177: The collected emission goes through a multimode fiber that is either routed to single-photon counting modules (Laser Components, Count T-100) or a spectrometer (Princeton Instruments, Iso-Plane160 and Pixis 100 CCD).
elements.sent-2411-178: For further details, see Patel et al., 2022.
elements.sent-2411-179: Analysis used to categorize emitter groups is based on previous work by Breitweiser et al., 2020.
elements.sent-2411-180: In this paper, we plot a histogram of the intensity of each pixel in the selected region (as indicated by dashed ovals in Figures \ref{fig:PL-effects and \ref{fig:PL-treatments ).}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Fits of the histogram determine the background level and each emitter group.
elements.sent-2411-182: We extract density and brightness parameters from the emitter groups.
elements.sent-2411-183: We select the number of distributions used based on the reduced chi squared value of the overall fit.
elements.sent-2411-184: TEM characterization was performed in the JEOL JEM-F200 S/TEM equipped with a Gatan OneView Camera and JEOL dual SDD detectors for EDS spectra acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-185: The JEOL F200 was operated at 80kV in both TEM mode and STEM mode.
elements.sent-2411-186: All TEM data were processed using Gatan DigitalMicrograph and ImageJ.
elements.sent-2411-187: Oxygen plasma irradiation treatment was performed on hBN samples in an Anatech SCE 108 Barrel Asher.
elements.sent-2411-188: TEM grids with hBN flakes were secured to a 4-inch, 500 $\mu$m Si and 1500 \r{A silicon oxide wafer using kapton tape.}
elements.sent-2411-189: This wafer was placed into the process chamber with oxygen plasma at 50W with flow rate of 25 or 50 sccm for 5 or 10 minutes, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-190: Annealing treatment was performed on hBN samples in the Carbolite HZS 1200\textdegree C 3 Zone Split Tube Furnace.
elements.sent-2411-191: The tube used is dedicated to hBN use only and cleaned prior to treatment with isopropyl alcohol and pressurized nitrogen gas.
elements.sent-2411-192: Samples are placed in an alumina ceramic boat and annealed in an Ar environment at 850\textdegree C for two hours.

elements.section-2411-009: Quantifying Changes in Contamination of Sample A
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/FigS2.png} \caption{Analysis of contamination coverage on Sample A: (a) Input TEM micrograph of Sample A after $\sim$ 40 minutes of electron irradiation. (b) Processed micrograph after cropping, enhancing contrast with CLAHE, applying a Gaussian blur and inverting the image for contouring. (c) Resulting binary mask after thresholding. (d) The same binary mask after filtering out small, contiguous groups of contaminated (white) pixels. (e) The final binary mask after applying a morphological filter to connect nearby regions of contamination. (f) Overlay of the final binary mask (yellow) on the cropped input micrograph. (g) Zoomed-in view of the region boxed in red in panel (f). (h) Distribution of discrete, contaminated areas shown in (f). } \label{fig:S2}
elements.sent-2411-216: TEM micrographs of Sample A show dark, lacey features (see Fig.~\MainFigTwo\ in the main text).
elements.sent-2411-217: These features are likely contamination resulting from the hBN transfer process, as indicated by EDS elemental maps.
elements.sent-2411-218: We identified and quantified these features according to the image processing routine outlined in Figure \ref{fig:S2 .}
elements.sent-2411-219: First, images are cropped and processed using contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) \cite{Pizer1987AdaptiveVariations to equalize local contrast and remove shadows.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Next, a Gaussian blur is applied to reduce noise and the image is inverted for contouring using OpenCV in Python \cite{Bradski2000OpenCV , shown in panel (b).}
elements.sent-2411-221: Contaminated areas are subsequently defined by thresholding the image to create a binary mask, shown in panel (c).
elements.sent-2411-222: The binary mask is improved by filtering out discrete, small contaminated areas below a certain size and using a morphological filter to connect nearby contaminated areas, shown in panel (d).
elements.sent-2411-223: The final mask can then be overlaid on the cropped input image (panel (f)) and used to compute a percent contamination coverage.
elements.sent-2411-224: Percent contamination coverage is defined as: \begin{equation}\% contamination coverage = \frac{number of contaminated (yellow) pixels}{total number of pixels} \times 100\%\end{equation} Based on this analysis, Sample A has a contamination coverage of 19\% after 40 minutes of electron irradiation.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\% contamination coverage = \frac{number of contaminated (yellow) pixels}{total number of pixels} \times 100\%\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-225: We also plotted the distribution of discrete, contaminated areas in Sample A, shown in panel (h) to better visualize changes in contamination over time.
elements.sent-2411-226: The image processing routine described above utilizes several user-defined parameters, which are summarized in the list below.
elements.sent-2411-228: Image cropping -- cropped image size, s = 220 $\times$ 220 nm$^2$ -- Images were cropped to only include the lower right quadrant to mitigate the effect of the overall background in the TEM images (see Fig. 2) which made quantification challenging.
elements.sent-2411-229: Cropping also reduced computation time since our processing routine is performed serially, pixel by pixel.
elements.sent-2411-230: CLAHE -- tile grid size, t = 2 $\times$ 2 nm$^2$ -- Tile grid size defines non-overlapping, regions of pixels (tiles) over which CLAHE is applied.
elements.sent-2411-231: A tile grid size of 2 $\times$ 2 nm$^2$ best enhanced contrast without significantly increasing noise in each image.
elements.sent-2411-232: CLAHE -- clip size, c = 1.1 -- clip size defines the maximum contrast enhancement allowed within a given tile.
elements.sent-2411-233: Gaussian blur -- kernel size, k$_{gb}$ = 0.3 $\times$ 0.3 nm$^2$ -- kernel size defines a window that slides across the image, applying the filter to each pixel and its neighbors within the window.
elements.sent-2411-234: Gaussian blur -- sigma, $\sigma$ = 0.03 nm -- Sigma defines the width of the Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-235: Thresholding -- threshold intensity, i = 175 -- Pixel intensity ranges from 0 (black) to 255 (white).
elements.sent-2411-236: Pixels with intensity greater than or equal to i become white, and pixels below i become black.
elements.sent-2411-237: Filtering minimum areas -- minimum area, $\text{a _{min}$ = 9 nm$^2$ -- This parameter defines a minimum area size for discrete contaminated regions.}
elements.sent-2411-238: Regions with areas below a$_{min}$ are set to 0 intensity.
elements.sent-2411-239: This step reduces noise in the final mask.
elements.sent-2411-240: Morphological closing filter -- kernel size, $\text{k _m$ = 1.0 $\times$ 1.0 nm$^2$ -- Similar to k$_{gb}$, k$_m$ defines a sliding window over which the morphological closing filter operates.}
elements.sent-2411-241: As this value increases, contaminated regions farther and farther apart are connected (and vice versa).
elements.sent-2411-242: As we varied the user-defined parameters across reasonable physical ranges, the results of our analysis only changed by a few percent.
elements.sent-2411-243: The set of parameters that best identified contaminated areas (determined by visual inspection) were ultimately used.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=0.75\linewidth]{figures/FigS3.png} \caption{Determining Morphological Filter Kernel Size and Minimum Area for Sample A: (a-c) Contaminated regions identified at 0, 25, and 40 minutes of electron irradiation for the first set of parameters. (d-f) Contaminated regions identified at 0, 25, and 40 minutes of electron irradiation for the second set of parameters. (g-i) Contaminated regions identified at 0, 25, and 40 minutes of electron irradiation for set (3) of parameters. (k) Bar chart showing the average percent coverage, across all three sets of parameters, and the corresponding standard deviations at each time. (l) Bar chart showing the average contaminated area size, across all three sets of parameters, and the corresponding standard deviations at each time.} \label{fig:S3}
elements.sent-2411-244: Figure \ref{fig:S3 demonstrates the parameter optimization process for parameters a$_{min}$ and k$_m$.}
elements.sent-2411-245: Three sets of a$_{min}$ and k$_m$ are shown in panels (a) through (i).
elements.sent-2411-246: The second set of parameters (a$_{min}$ = 9 nm$^2$ and k$_m$ = 1.0 nm), boxed in green, was chosen as the optimal set of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-247: The first set of parameters (a$_{min}$ = 5.6 nm$^2$ and k$_m$ = 1.1 nm) tended to overestimate contamination (by including noise) and the third set of parameters (a$_{min}$ = 5.6 nm$^2$ and k$_m$ = 0.8 nm) tended to underestimate contamination (by leaving neighboring contaminated regions disjoint).
elements.sent-2411-248: Note that additional combinations of a$_{min}$ and k$_m$, and other parameters not shown here, were tested and compared in a similar fashion.) Panels (k) and (j) in Figure \ref{fig:S3 show that for different combinations of parameters, percent coverage of contamination and the average size of contaminated regions peak at 25 minutes and are at their lowest at 40 minutes.}

elements.section-2411-012: TEM and Treatment Effects on Optical Activity
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/FigS7.png} \caption{PL Measurements of Samples B \& C: (a) Initial PL image for Sample B. (b) PL image of Sample B after TEM measurement. (c) PL image of Sample B after oxygen plasma irradiation. (d) Initial PL image for Sample C. (d) PL image of Sample C after TEM measurement. elements.sent-2411-259: PL images were taken of Samples B and C before and after the TEM imaging and treatments shown in Figure 4 of the main text.
elements.sent-2411-260: The initial PL images show the typical localized emission (Fig.~\ref{fig:PL-BC a,d).}
elements.sent-2411-261: The optical activity changes significantly after exposure to the electron beam (Fig.~\ref{fig:PL-BC b,e).}
elements.sent-2411-262: The overall brightness greatly increases, and no localized emitters are visible.
elements.sent-2411-263: The edge of the FIB hole also brightens considerably.
elements.sent-2411-264: Notably, none of these effects occur when a sample is placed in the TEM column under vacuum without electron beam exposure (see Section~\ref{sec:TEM-vacuum ).}

elements.sent-2411-266: Following the measurements shown in Figures~\ref{fig:PL-BC b,e, Samples B and C were treated by oxygen plasma irradiation and annealing, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-267: The measurements shown in Figures~\ref{fig:PL-BC c,f were taken after treatment but before the "after" TEM measurements shown in Figure \MainFigFour.}
elements.sent-2411-268: After oxygen irradiation, the background brightness of Sample B returns to the same level as the initial image, but no localized emission is visible (Fig.~\ref{fig:PL-BC c).}

elements.figure-2411-016: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/FigS8.png} \caption{PL measurements of alternate oxygen treatment recipe: (a) Initial PL image of Sample F. (b) PL image of Sample F after oxygen plasma irradiation. (c) Emitter family analysis from (a) and (b).} \label{fig:alt-oxygen}
elements.sent-2411-275: Sample F was exposed to oxygen plasma of 50 W at rate of 25 sccm for 10 mins instead of 50 sccm for 5 mins.
elements.sent-2411-276: The initial PL image shows that emission is localized and mostly occurs along extended defects (Fig.~\ref{fig:alt-oxygen a).}
elements.sent-2411-277: Much of this emission is no longer visible after the oxygen plasma treatment, but a new extended defect appears with bright emission (Fig.~\ref{fig:alt-oxygen b).}
elements.sent-2411-278: Emitter family analysis shows that there is little change in overall optical activity (Fig.~\ref{fig:alt-oxygen c).}
elements.sent-2411-279: This result is in contrast to the oxygen treatment used on Sample D (Fig. \MainFigPLTreatments a-c).
elements.sent-2411-280: While the 5 minute treatment results in brightened, diffuse emission, the 10 minute treatment does not result in significant change.
elements.sent-2411-281: One explanation is that this alternate recipe does not damage the material as much because of the lower flow rate.
elements.sent-2411-282: This notion is further demonstrated by the change in sample thickness due to the treatment.
elements.sent-2411-283: Sample B (50 sccm, 5 min) thinned by $\sim$6 nm, while Sample F (25 sccm, 10 min) thinned by $\sim$3 nm.
elements.sent-2411-284: Another factor to consider is that the barrel asher heats over time, so it is likely that temperature contributes to the difference in result between the two recipes.
elements.sent-2411-285: This suggests that there may be an optimal set of irradiation parameters which may clean organic residue from flakes without etching the underlying hBN.
elements.sent-2411-286: Hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) plays a central role in nanoelectronics and nanophotonics.
elements.sent-2411-287: Moreover, hBN hosts room-temperature quantum emitters and optically addressable spins, making it promising for quantum sensing and quantum photonics.
elements.sent-2411-288: Despite many investigations of their optical properties, however, the emitters’ chemical structure remains unclear, as does the role of contamination at surfaces and interfaces in forming the emitters or modifying their properties.
elements.sent-2411-289: We prepare hBN samples that are compatible with confocal photoluminescence (PL) microscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and atomic-force microscopy (AFM), and we use those techniques to quantitatively investigate correlations between fluorescent emission, flake morphology, and surface residue.
elements.sent-2411-290: The methods can be broadly applied to study two-dimensional materials, and the results illustrate the importance of correlative studies to elucidate structural factors that influence hBN's functionality as a host for quantum emitters and spin defects.
elements.sent-2411-291: Hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) is a van der Waals material with broad utility.
elements.sent-2411-292: As a wide-bandgap semiconductor ($\sim$5.9 eV) \cite{Shaik2021, Jungwirth2016 , hBN serves a key role as a dielectric layer in electronic devices \cite{Dean2010BoronElectronics,Yu2013InteractionCapacitance,Kuiri2015ProbingMeasurements .}}
elements.sent-2411-293: In addition, hBN is poised to play a key role in quantum information science, since the material hosts bright quantum emitters at room temperature \cite{Tran2016b,Exarhos2017,Mendelson2019EngineeringNitride,Patel2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-294: Some emitters display spin-dependent optical properties and can be utilized as spin qubits \cite{Exarhos2019,Chejanovsky2021,Guo2023CoherentTemperature,Patel2024RoomNitride,Stern2024AConditions .}
elements.sent-2411-295: However, hBN's quantum emitters are not yet fully understood.
elements.sent-2411-296: Identifying emitters in two-dimensional materials is especially complex because they are sensitive to interfaces \cite{Exarhos2017,Akbari2021 and other local environmental effects (e.g., defects \cite{Chejanovsky2021 , strain \cite{Proscia2018,Sajid2020 , and contamination \cite{Yang2022EffectMaterials,Li2023ProlongedEmitters ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-297: Understanding and controlling the properties of hBN, especially at its surface, are therefore crucial to advancing quantum information and photonics applications.
elements.sent-2411-298: Techniques such as photoluminescence (PL) microscopy and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) are commonly employed to characterize hBN.
elements.sent-2411-299: PL provides information about optical properties, including absorption and emission characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-300: For the study of quantum emitters, it provides essential chemical and electronic information via the spectra of electronic and vibronic transitions \cite{Jungwirth2016,Exarhos2017,Wigger2019 , as well as spatial information regarding the emitters' density and brightness \cite{Breitweiser2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-301: SEM has been used to study surface topography and morphology \cite{Exarhos2017,Mendelson2019EngineeringNitride , in addition to activating emission centers in hBN \cite{Tran2016b,Breitweiser2020,Fournier2021Position-controlledNitride .}}
elements.sent-2411-302: More recently, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has been used as a means of probing quantum emission in hBN, particularly when paired with PL or cathodoluminescence spectroscopy \cite{Hayee2020,Li2023ProlongedEmitters .}
elements.sent-2411-303: While the optical properties and material structure of hBN are often studied separately, few studies have investigated them in parallel.
elements.sent-2411-304: Confocal PL studies in the visible-wavelength range are typically limited by diffraction to a resolution of $\sim$300 nm, whereas TEM studies can achieve atomic resolution.
elements.sent-2411-305: By combining both of these techniques, the capabilities of TEM can be leveraged to elucidate the structural and chemical composition of quantum emitters identified via PL microscopy.
elements.figure-2411-017: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{figures/Fig1.png} \caption{ Summary of Experimental Methods HBN samples undergo an iterative process that includes basic characterization, correlative PL and TEM measurements, and treatments. (a) All samples are first characterized with white light imaging and AFM. elements.sent-2411-306: In this work, we combine PL and TEM imaging together with atomic-force microscopy (AFM) to bridge the gap in understanding between hBN's optical properties and material structure.
elements.sent-2411-307: We study changes in optical activity and flake morphology associated with different measurements and treatments, focusing on overall statistical properties of the material rather than investigating individual single-photon emitters with PL or few-atom defects with TEM.
elements.sent-2411-308: To this end, we fabricate custom substrates compatible with both microscopies onto which hBN flakes are transferred with thicknesses ranging from $\sim$10--30 nm.
elements.sent-2411-309: First, we consider the effects of PL and TEM measurements themselves.
elements.sent-2411-310: Using quantitative methods, we show that exposure to the electron beam during TEM imaging induces changes to surface contaminants and that PL imaging induces photobleaching.
elements.sent-2411-311: Subsequently, we study correlated changes in structure and optical activity following annealing and oxygen plasma, treatments commonly applied to hBN \cite{Li2019PurificationTreatments,Li2023ProlongedEmitters,Venturi2024SelectiveNitride,Shaik2021,Mohajerani2024NarrowbandNitride, Chen2023AnnealingNitride .}
elements.sent-2411-312: Both treatments significantly alter sample morphology and optical activity.
elements.sent-2411-313: We discuss the implications of this work for understanding and engineering quantum emitters in hBN, as well as for future investigations of other two-dimensional materials.
elements.sent-2411-314: Figure \ref{fig:Schematic summarizes the sample geometry along with the different characterization methods and treatments we explore in this work.}
elements.sent-2411-315: We report results for six hBN samples, labeled A through F, each of which received a distinct sequence of measurements and treatments that are summarized in Table~\TabSampleList\ in the Supporting Information.
elements.sent-2411-316: A 5- to 10-$\mu$m-diameter FIB hole (Fig.~\ref{fig:Schematic a) provides a sufficiently large region of suspended hBN suitable for both TEM and PL microscopy.}

elements.sent-2411-318: Flakes of the desired thickness are identified by optical contrast prior to transferring and subsequently confirmed with AFM (see Tables \TabThickness--\TabRoughness\ and Figs.~\FigAllAFM--\FigRoughness\ in the Supporting Information).

elements.sent-2411-414: In this work, we demonstrated a method for sequential, correlated TEM and PL measurements of multi-layer hBN, and we used these complementary techniques, along with AFM and white light imaging, to study the interplay between surface contamination, overall morphology, flake thickness, and optical properties of mechanically exfoliated hBN\,---\,all critical considerations towards scalable quantum information and photonics technologies utilizing hBN.
elements.sent-2411-415: We observe that the residue exhibits non-monotonic changes in structure, and the optical emission exhibits non-monotonic changes in brightness as a result of electron irradiation.
elements.sent-2411-416: In these samples, all optical emission is unstable and subject to photobleaching, potentially due to the flake thickness.
elements.sent-2411-417: We find that oxygen plasma treatment results in thinning of hBN and reduces surface residue while brightening and diffusing PL emission.
elements.sent-2411-418: In contrast, annealing induces warping and cracking in hBN flakes and results in an increase or re-distribution of surface residue while reducing optical activity overall.
elements.sent-2411-419: These results contradict previous assumptions that such treatments clean the surface by removing residue without inflicting damage on the hBN, highlighting the need for further investigations of this subject.
elements.sent-2411-420: Such work could include a systematic comparison of different sample preparation methods (i.e., dry transfer, wet transfer, etc.) and investigations of the resulting residue or lack thereof; a systematic comparison of the effect of thickness on optical activity, especially in combination with thermal annealing; fine-tuning of oxygen plasma parameters to optimally clean surfaces without damaging the hBN matrix; and investigation of large-scale morphological (e.g., strain-induced) effects by utilizing different sample substrates and geometries and annealing recipes.
elements.sent-2411-421: Motivated by the broad and interdisciplinary use of hBN, such studies can significantly advance the realization of new nanoelectronic and nanophotonic technologies.
elements.sent-2411-422: Likewise for use in quantum information science and photonics, quantitative understanding and control of surface contamination is essential to engineering materials and devices.
elements.sent-2411-423: By correlating optical and structural imaging, it will become possible to characterize and potentially even create quantum emitters at the atomic scale, facilitating the design and realization of new generations of quantum technologies based on hBN.
elements.sent-2411-424: Moreover, the methods and procedures introduced in this work can be directly adapted to study the structural and optical properties of a broad range of 2D materials.

elements.figure-2411-029: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{figures/FigS4.png} \caption{Analysis of contamination coverage on Sample A: (a) Input TEM micrograph of Sample A after $\sim$ 40 minutes of electron irradiation. (d) The same binary mask after filtering out small, contiguous groups of contaminated (white) pixels. (e) The final binary mask after applying a morphological filter to connect nearby regions of contamination. (f) Overlay of the final binary mask (yellow) on the cropped input micrograph. (g) Zoomed-in view of the region boxed in red in panel (f). (h) Distribution of discrete, contaminated areas shown in (f). } \label{fig:S2}
elements.sent-2411-443: Image cropping -- cropped image size, s = 220 $\times$ 220 nm$^2$ -- Images were cropped to only include the lower right quadrant to mitigate the effect of the overall background in the TEM images (see Fig.~2) which made quantification challenging.
elements.sent-2411-444: CLAHE -- tile grid size, t = 2 $\times$ 2 nm$^2$ -- Tile grid size defines non-overlapping regions of pixels (tiles) over which CLAHE is applied.
